llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: This is a natural experiment using difference-in-differences. Python packages gained access to GitHub Copilot (LLM coding assistance) from October 2021, while R packages did not have Copilot access during the study period. The python_treated arm represents human+AI (developers with Copilot), and r_control represents human-only baseline (developers without AI assistance). The primary comparison is the cross-language control design (Python vs R).
llm_extracted.notes: This is a difference-in-differences natural experiment. The primary specification (TWFE) reports a regression coefficient of 6.816 commits per quarter increase (p<0.001), representing a 39.55% increase. The outcome is in levels (commits per quarter), not log-transformed. The pre-treatment mean for the treated group (Python packages before Copilot) is 17.235 commits per quarter, which serves as the baseline. Multiple robustness checks are reported (balanced panel: 5.496 commits/31.89%; synthetic DiD: 4.894 commits/28.40%), and a temporal control design using only Python packages across eras confirms the effect (4.638 commits/27.13%). Using the primary TWFE specification with the baseline mean allows computation of log ratio as log(17.235 + 6.816) / log(17.235). The Rust vs Haskell comparison is an additional robustness check but Python vs R is the primary analysis.
source_quotes: GitHub Copilot increases commits by 6.816 per quarter in the TWFE specification (p<0.001), a 39.55% increase relative to the pre-treatment mean of the treated group of 17.235 commits per quarter
The effect is consistent across specifications: 5.496 commits (31.89%) in the balanced panel and 4.894 commits (28.40%) in synthetic DiD.
Python packages with access to GitHub Copilot from October 2021 onwards. GitHub Copilot, an LLM-powered coding assistance tool, initially supported Python from the start.

R packages without access to GitHub Copilot during the study period. R was not supported by Copilot; at least until the ChatGPT launch in late 2022, R packages did not have access to mainstream LLM coding assistance.
